fields.Name: Common Threads Summit 2026: The Next Horizon

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: Treaty, rights and representation
fields.Bio: Ngarra Murray is a proud Wamba Wamba, Yorta Yorta, Dja Dja Wurrung and Dhudhuroa woman. She is a respected Aboriginal leader, treaty practitioner, and advocate for Indigenous self-determination. Ngarra is a former Co-Chair and inaugural member of the First Peoples' Assembly of Victoria, serving two terms and playing a central leadership role in guiding Victoria through Australia's first modern treaty-making process. As a signatory to the first modern treaty in Australia, Ngarra provided steady, principled leadership during a defining period in Victoria's history - helping shape the structures, frameworks, and relationships required to progress treaty negotiations between First Peoples and the State, and contributing to a foundation for future agreements across the continent. Beyond Victoria, Ngarra contributes to international Indigenous engagement, recognising the long-standing relationships and shared experiences of Indigenous peoples globally. Her work reflects a deep commitment to Indigenous-led diplomacy, solidarity between nations, and strengthening First Peoples' influence in local, national and international forums.
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 2 morning plenary on Treaty, rights and representation.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: Climate justice and protecting Country
fields.Bio: Professor Anne Poelina PhD, PhD, MEd, MPH&TM, MA is a Nyikina Warrwa woman from the Kimberley region of Western Australia. An active community leader, human and earth rights advocate, poet and film maker. Chair Indigenous Knowledges and Senior Research Fellow Nulungu Institute Research University of Notre Dame, Adjunct Professor, College of Indigenous Education Futures, Arts & Society, Charles Darwin University, Darwin. Adjunct Professor School of Social Sciences, University of Western Australia, Visiting Fellow Crawford School of Public Policy at the Australian National University, Canberra. Elected Chair of the Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council (2018).
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 2 afternoon plenary on climate justice and protecting Country.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: the moment we are in
fields.Bio: Celeste is an Arrernte women, a union organiser, a writer, an activist and a social commentator. Over decades now, she has been involved in multiple movements, and has additionally used her skills of discernment and relatability, to bring debates and observation to the media.
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 1 opening plenary.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Leeroy Bilney is a proud Barngala, Kuprun, Wirangu, Kokatha, Mirning and Noongar man from Ceduna, South Australia. A nationally recognised leader across Indigenous philanthropy, governance, and community development, Leeroy brings deep experience spanning Aboriginal community-controlled organisations, government, and cross-sector reform. His work is grounded in strengthening Indigenous leadership, building community capacity, and driving systemic change. He is the Regional Manager (Australia) for the International Funders for Indigenous Peoples and Co-Founder and Director of the Spinifex Foundation SA. As the inaugural Co-Presiding Member of South Australia's First Nations Voice to Parliament, Leeroy continues to shape policy and advocacy at the highest levels. Drawing on storytelling and lived experience, he engages audiences to think differently, act with purpose, and invest in Indigenous-led futures.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Kuruma Marthedunera woman Josie Alec is the First Nations Lead for Australian Conservation Foundation, the co-founder of Save our Songlines. "I have been blessed with an extraordinary life journey. From stolen generation, to growing up with a wonderful foster family, to making my way back home to my amazing family and culture." Spiritualism runs strong in both of my families and was the grounding I needed upon my return home. My Mum was a traditional healer and held many sacred gifts and knowledge about our country. Through that knowledge I was taught about our native ecology and the traditional use of native plants, their purpose and the connection to everything around them. Our Lore and Culture has played a big part in the rejuvenation and longevity for centuries in my country and informs the way we connect and live our daily lives. I became a teacher in 2013 and gained many skills within the community I lived in and bought my children up back on their country. I co-founded Save our Songlines which is a campaign we started to make people aware of the risk to our country and the sacred rock art that has been there for over 60,000 years. My niece and I went to Geneva to speak on behalf of our country and cultural practises at the United Nations First Nations conference in 2022. As the First nations Lead at ACF we created a documentary called Heart of Country, which explores different First Nations people across Australia and their plight to save their country. I am pleased to share my journey about learning how important our connection with country is and the care for country practices, as first nations people we share. Looking after the environment is key to our future.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Karina Lester is a Yankunytjatjara Woman from the Anangu Pitjantjatjara Yankunytjatjara Lands (APY Lands), in far north west South Australia. Karina is the Co-Manager and Senior Aboriginal Language Expert for the Mobile Language Team and is a speaker, interpreter and translator of her Yankunytjatjara Wangka (language). Karina is also a second-generation survivor of the British Nuclear Tests at Emu Field in 1953 and the daughter of Anti-Nuc activist Yami Lester. She is also an Ambassador for the International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons (ICAN Australia) speaking strong about the harms felt by her people.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Professor Jackie Huggins AM FAHA is Bidjara and BirriGubba Juru. She is currently Director of Partnerships and Innovation, Faculty of Health, Medicine and Behavioural Sciences, University of Queensland, Herston campus, Brisbane. Elder in Residence, Australian Broadcasting Commission Elder in Residence, Australian Progress and and Common Threads Co-Chair, National Apology Foundation Professor Huggins is in demand as a speaker, mentor, writer, advisor with over four decades experience in community, academia, government and non government sectors.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Opening remarks
fields.Description: Delivered opening remarks in the Day 1 introduction to Common Threads.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: Panel discussion contributor
fields.Bio: Mililma May is a Danggalaba Kulumbringin Tiwi Rak Thanggural woman from Gulumoerrgin (so-called Darwin) in the Northern Territory, Australia. She is a writer and community organiser working across justice, movement building, and storytelling. She is the Executive Officer of Movement Building & Advocacy at Change the Record, and the former CEO and Co-Founder of Uprising of the People. Mililma has supported grassroots movements across the Northern Territory, including Shut Down Don Dale, Save Lee Point, and Stop Middle Arm. Her work is grounded in political analysis, deep historical awareness, and the authority of Aboriginal law and governance. Her writing has appeared in Cheek Media, Crikey, NITV, Kindling + Sage, and We Are Explorers.
fields.Description: Panel discussion contributor in the Day 1 afternoon plenary on frontline movements for justice.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: International guest speaker
fields.Bio: Taily Terena is an indigenous woman from the Terena Nation, from the Pantanal, the wetlands of Brazil. A land defender and climate advocate, she has been working for more than 15 years for the rights of Indigenous Peoples, focusing on indigenous women and youth. As an anthropologist, she has studied the history of her people through intergenerational knowledge and decolonization. Part of the Terena youth collective, Inamati Xane, she currently works with topics related to agroecology, climate change, and territory. She works at the IITC - International Indian Treaty Council in the Environmental Health and Food Sovereignty programme.
fields.Description: International guest speaker in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Duane is a Wulgurukaba and Bidjara man, raised between Tennant Creek and Magnetic Island. For more than a decade his work has been rooted in Country, the Great Barrier Reef and the community-based programs that care for it, and grounded in a singular conviction: Country and People are one, and Traditional Owners must hold the power and capacity to safeguard their biocultural land and seascapes, their heritage, and their cultural expression. That work has taken him across senior statutory and community boards spanning regional development, marine and environmental science, environmental regulation, and tourism. He currently serves as CEO of the Council of First Nations and Co-Chair of ReefTO Taskforce. When mob stand shoulder to shoulder, organise, and prosecute our priorities collectively, we speak from a position of power, not permission. Unity is not a slogan. It is the foundation for the next chapter.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Content Body:
Common Threads Summit is committed to a safe, inclusive and respectful environment for all participants.

We expect everyone to engage in good faith, listen respectfully and avoid harassment, discrimination or harmful conduct.

If you experience or witness a concern, contact the summit safety team immediately.

Use strong passwords and multi-factor authentication on devices used during summit days.

Do not share personal or sensitive details in public channels.

Report suspicious digital activity to summit crew straight away.
